summary: Julia Evans documents her progress building an SSH game project using Firecracker VMs. She shares three accomplishments: understanding why Linux bridges are confusing (they're actually two things bundled together), fixing slow Ubuntu VM boot times caused by systemd entropy generation, and writing a Go HTTP server to manage Firecracker VM lifecycles. The post captures a day of hands-on systems work with honest accounts of confusion and pragmatic workarounds.
key_points: Linux bridges are confusing because they bundle two things: an actual bridge (like a switch) and a network interface with an IP address, which switches don't have
Ubuntu Firecracker VMs were slow to boot due to systemd's random seed service; after six failed fix attempts, setting a 2-second timeout worked
She wrote a simple Go HTTP API to create and destroy Firecracker VMs, largely by adapting code from the firectl command-line tool
The post demonstrates a pragmatic approach to systems work: accepting imperfect solutions and moving forward
money_quotes: Every single time I say I'm confused about bridges someone will tell me "well, you see julia, a bridge is like a virtual switch". This has never made any sense to me because I've never used a switch either.
But switches don't have IP addresses! So if "a bridge is like a switch", what's going on?
When you create a bridge on Linux by default, you actually get 2 things: a bridge (the kind that's "like a switch") and a network interface with the same name as the bridge, which has an IP address.
I feel kind of vindicated in disbelieving this "a bridge is like a switch" explanation because I guess it's technically true but it's definitely missing a key piece of information.

It's extremely possible that I actually need this entropy generation for some reason (maybe to give sshd enough entropy so that it can generate session keys securely?) but I'll cross that bridge when I come to it.
So now I can start an Ubuntu virtual machine in like 5 seconds! It's really amazing.
key_insight: Common technical explanations like 'a bridge is like a switch' often fail because they omit crucial implementation details that would actually help people understand the concept.
